title_en: The Chain of Transmission — Its Importance and Unique Status in Islam
content_en:
The concluding chapter of this section of the Muqaddimah addresses the most distinctive feature of Islamic hadith scholarship — the system of connected chains of transmission (isnad) — and reflects on its unique place in human civilization and its indispensability for the religious life of the Muslim community.

Ibn al-Salah cites the celebrated statement of Imam Abdullah ibn al-Mubarak (118–181 AH): 'The isnad is part of the religion. If not for the isnad, anyone could say whatever they wanted.' This statement captures the essential function of the isnad: it is not an antiquarian curiosity but a living guarantee of the authenticity of religious knowledge. Without the ability to trace every narration back through named individuals to the Prophet ﷺ himself, Islam would be vulnerable to the same textual corruption that affected the earlier scriptures — a point that Imam al-Shafi'i made explicitly in his landmark work al-Risalah.

The early Muslim scholars recognized the Islamic intellectual tradition's unique contribution to human knowledge in the form of the isnad. The great hadith master Muhammad ibn Sirin (33–110 AH) is reported to have said: 'This knowledge is religion, so look carefully from whom you take your religion.' The emphasis on knowing the identity and character of one's teachers — not merely the content of what they teach — is a form of intellectual accountability with no parallel in any other civilization's scholarly tradition of the same era.

The isnad system operated through a social and institutional infrastructure of remarkable sophistication: the networks of scholars who traveled vast distances to receive narrations directly from those who had heard them (al-rihla fi talab al-hadith); the dictation sessions (imla') in which scholars narrated hadiths to assembled students who wrote them down; the certificate systems (ijazah) that formally certified a student's right to transmit a teacher's material; and the biographical dictionaries (kutub al-rijal) that recorded the names, biographies, and scholarly evaluations of tens of thousands of transmitters across more than two centuries of oral transmission.

The practical and theological significance of the isnad for the contemporary Muslim is multi-dimensional. At the most obvious level, it grounds Islamic religious practice in an authenticated connection to the Prophet ﷺ. But at a deeper level, the isnad embodies the Islamic concept of 'silsilah' — a chain of transmission that connects each generation of Muslims to the one before it, all the way back to the Companions and the Prophet ﷺ himself. The Muslim who reads a hadith from Sahih al-Bukhari is not reading a historical document in the ordinary sense — they are receiving, through an unbroken chain of witnesses, the words of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. The isnad is the institutional form of the Islamic community's identity as an ummah defined by its connection to its Prophet ﷺ.

The closing pages of the Muqaddimah's section on isnad make a point that Ibn al-Salah considers essential: the science of hadith criticism is not a destructive or skeptical enterprise but a protective one. Every effort to identify weak narrators and unreliable chains is an effort to preserve what is authentic. The hadith critics were not cynics who doubted the possibility of authentic prophetic transmission; they were believers who cared so deeply about that transmission that they devoted their lives to ensuring its integrity.
